figures.caption: Fig. 3: Empirical evaluation summary. (a,b) Fairness–latency frontier at n = 50 for Starlink and Internet:
Table 8: Robustness of LIA (λ = 1 s−1) to bounded slack-estimation error at n = 50 (mean over Starlink and Internet).
Table 1: Positioning LIA relative to other latency-fairness design classes.
Table 5: Observed slack-spread summaries at n = 50 under the paper’s horizon policy (1,000 sampled instances per topology).
Table 6: Large-market (n = 1000) performance by topology. Entries are means with 95% bootstrap CIs over 1,000 instances per topology. Compute time is winner determination time on a single CPU core.

Figure 1: Spacetime view of two bids. Dashed lines are light-cone boundaries under unit aspect (c=1). The shaded rectangle marks a causal slab where bids are incomparable; the red line is the frontier where horizon-discounted bids are compared (i.e., where θ1e −λδ1 = θ2e −λδ2). Its position depends on the values (θ1, θ2), the parameter λ, and how each bid’s horizon slack δi changes with emission time. Proper-time lapses δi (horizon slacks) are measured along each worldline from emission to the clearing horizon H , with δi ≡ ∆τi.
